coredata.dc:title: A fluorescence “turn-on” sensor for ascorbic acid in fruit juice and beverage based on ascorbate oxidase-like activity of citric acid-derived carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Zhen
Zeng, Qi
Yao, Wensong
Chen, Wei
Cai, Chuangui
Yang, Jialin
Lin, Xinhua
Chen, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Citric acid-derived carbon dots (CA-CDs) without any modifications were found to have the ascorbate oxidase (AAO)-like activity. The CA-CDs have high affinity for ascorbic acid (AA), which is similar to natural AAO. The robustness of CA-CDs is greater than that of AAO. Based on the AAO mimetic activity of CA-CDs, a sensitive turn-on mode and natural enzyme-free fluorescence detection method has been developed for AA in some fruit juice and beverage samples with satisfied recoveries. This study provides CDs-based AAO mimetic nanozymes to replace the expensive natural enzymes or heavy metal-based nanozymes, which will show great potential in biological and food assays.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ascorbic acid
Carbon dots
Ascorbate oxidase
Food assays

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of AA in juice and beverage
The specificity of the method and actual sample analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a and b) HRTEM image of CA-CDs. (c) 13C NMR spectroscopy of CA and CA-CDs. (d) AAO-like activity of CA-CDs.
Catalytic mechanism of CA-CDs: (a) The participation of oxygen. (b) The generation of DHAA. (c) The production of H2O2. (d) Fluorescence of H2O2-TA and H2O2-TA-AA system.
(a) Steady-state dynamic analysis of CA-CDs. (b) Dose-response curve for detecting AA. (c) Standard curve of AA. (d) Fluorescence intensity response of AA and interferences.
Schematic diagram of AA determination based on the AAO-like activity of CA-CDs.
Comparison of fluorescence methods for AA detection.
Analytical results for AA contents of juice and beverage sample determined using the CA-CDs sensor and the standard method.

head.ce:title: A fluorescence “turn-on” sensor for ascorbic acid in fruit juice and beverage based on ascorbate oxidase-like activity of citric acid-derived carbon dots

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Citric acid-derived carbon dots (CA-CDs) with excellent ascorbate oxidase-like activity and catalyze the oxidation of ascorbic acid to produce dehydroascorbic acid and H2O2. Ascorbic acid can further accelerate the decomposition of H2O2 to produce OH•, and OH• can change TA to TA-OH with fluorescent emission.

Citric acid-derived carbon dots (CA-CDs) without any modifications were found to have the ascorbate oxidase (AAO)-like activity. The CA-CDs have high affinity for ascorbic acid (AA), which is similar to natural AAO. The robustness of CA-CDs is greater than that of AAO. Based on the AAO mimetic activity of CA-CDs, a sensitive turn-on mode and natural enzyme-free fluorescence detection method has been developed for AA in some fruit juice and beverage samples with satisfied recoveries. This study provides CDs-based AAO mimetic nanozymes to replace the expensive natural enzymes or heavy metal-based nanozymes, which will show great potential in biological and food assays.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA), as a highly effective antioxidant, has strong reducing properties and participates in the synthesis and metabolism of many important biomolecules in the body (Liu, & Pang, 2018; Gan, Su, Chen, & Yang, 2020). The human body itself cannot synthesize AA. It is necessary to supplement AA through exogenous food intake. However, AA is easily degraded during storage and heating. Therefore, the determination of ascorbic acid in food samples has received great attention.

In the past decade, fluorescence (He et al., 2020), colorimetry (Chandra et al., 2019) chemiluminescence (CL) (Dong, Gao, Chu, Chen, & Wang, 2014), and electrochemical methods (de Faria et al., 2020) have been developed for the determination of AA. Most of the methods for AA detection are based on the reductive properties of AA. AA reacted with the oxidant species in CL or colorimetric system and quenched the CL or Ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) absorption signal to give a concentration-dependent response (Wang, Halawa, Lou, Gao, Li, & Xu, 2021; Chi, Zhu, Jing, Wang, & Lu, 2019). However, the “on–off” detection model for AA is susceptible to environmental influences. “on–off-on” switchable sensors were the other strategy to detect AA (Liu, Chen, Tang, Wang, Kang, & Yao, 2015; Luo et al., 2018). Li’s group used Fe3+ to quench the original fluorescence of N and S co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) (off). AA then reduced Fe3+ to Fe2+ and recovered the FL of N, S-CDs (on). This switchable sensor has been used to detect AA in fruit. However, the AA of fruit is unstable and prone to oxidative decomposition.

Ascorbic acid oxidase (AAO) is a multi-copper oxidase that reduces oxygen to water in the presence of oxygen, and AA is oxidized to dehydroascorbic acid (DHAA) (Wang et al., 2019). Commonly, natural AAO comes from plants, fungi, and insects (F Abdelgawad, El-Mogy, Mohamed, Garchery, & G Stevens, 2019; Braunschmid et al., 2020; Peng et al., 2015). Natural AAO is expensive and has poor stability. Some metal-contained nanozymes, such as Au@Pt NRs (Liu et al., 2012), copper oxide nanoparticles (Chen et al., 2012) have been developed as memetic AAO, to be an alternative to natural AAO. However, the further application of the metal contained nanozymes was hindered by their stability in severe pH media. The exploration of metal-free AAO mimicking is of significant importance.

In the present work, we found AAO-like activity of citric-derived carbon dots (CA-CDs) without metal atoms or heteroatoms doping, The CA-CDs exhibited a similar affinity towards AA to that of natural AAO and the CA-CDs is stable in wide pH range and avoids the pollutant from heavy metal ions. The CA-CDs catalyze the oxidation of AA to DHAA with the generation of H2O2. Based on the AAO-like activity of CA-CDs, this research provides a natural enzyme-free “turn-on” sensor for AA in juice and beverage samples. The proposed sensor for AA is sensitive, stable, and facile. Furthermore, CA-CDs can be easily prepared through a simple direct solvothermal method.

Citric acid, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %), terephthalic acid (TA), sodium acetate trihydrate, acetic acid, l-serine, d-threonine, l-alanine, l-cysteine, Lysine, proline, glutathione, glucose, maltose, KCl, and NaOH were bought from Aladdin reagent Co. Ltd. AA, o-Phenylenediamine (OPDA), NaCl, HCl were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Dehydroascorbic acid (DHAA), dialysis bags, and catalase (CAT) were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Tomato, strawberry, kiwi, peach beverage, and lemon beverage were obtained from a local supermarket..

The morphologies of CA-CDs were detected by a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM-2100F). The accelerating voltage for HRTEM is 200 kV. The UV–vis absorption spectra were monitored by a cary-300 spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA) using a quartz cell with a 1 cm light path. The fluorescence spectrum was recorded by a Cary Eclipse spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA). 13C NMR spectra of the CA-CDs were measured by a TA Bruker 600 MHz Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectrometer.

CA-CDs are synthesized by hydrothermal method. The steps of the synthesis are listed in the supplementary literature. A fluorescent method was used to investigate the AAO mimic activity of CDs. 200 µL of 1.5 mM AA was mixed with 50 µL of 7.06 mg/mL CA-CDs in a water bath at 37 ℃ for 20 min. 500 µL of 40 mM TA and 3.25 mL of acetate buffer (pH 5.0, 0.2 M) were added to the mixture of AA and CA-CDs. The solution was then incubated at 55 ℃ for 15 min, and an ice bath for 1 min. The fluorescence spectra of the above solution with different reaction times (4, 8, 12, 16, and 20 min) were measured with an excitation wavelength of 315 nm. Taking the reaction time as the abscissa and the fluorescence intensity as the ordinate, a straight line can be obtained by plotting. By changing the concentration of AA, a series of straight lines and their slopes can be obtained. A graph was drawn with the AA concentration as the abscissa and the slope as the ordinate. Km was obtained through the fitting of Michaelis equation 1/ν = Km /V max (1/[S] + 1/Km ).

200 µL of AA with different concentrations and 50 µL of 7.06 mg/mL CA-CDs were mixed in a water bath at 37 ℃ for 20 min. 500 µL of 40 mM TA and 3.25 mL of acetate buffer (pH 5.0, 0.2 M) were then added to the above solution. The mixed solution was placed in a water bath at 55 ℃ for 15 min, and an ice bath for 1 min. Finally, its fluorescence spectrum was recorded with an excitation wavelength of 315 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of AA in juice and beverage
ce:section.ce:para: Tomato, strawberry, kiwi juice, and two commercial peach and lemon beverage were selected as actual samples for the detection of AA. AA was extracted from fresh fruit samples according to literature methods (Luo et al., 2018). The fruit was weighed, then mashed, mixed with an equal volume of 0.2 g/L oxalic acid, and centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 20 min at 4 °C. After filtering, the extraction of the tomato, strawberry, and kiwi fruit samples was diluted 10 times with oxalic acid, respectively. Two kinds of different commercial peach and lemon beverage were diluted 10 times with acetate buffer.

The detection results of AA in juice and beverage by the proposed method and the standard method have been compared through the F-test and t-test. Standard addition experiments have been carried out to verify the practical applicability of the proposed method.

Pancreatic cancer cells (Panc-1) and cervical cancer cells (Hela) were seeded into 96-well plates at 5 × 103 cells per well and cultured in an incubator at 37 °C, 5 % CO2 for 24 h. Different concentrations of CA-CDs (25, 50, 100, 200, and 400 µg/ml) were added to each well and cultured for 24 h for the MTT experiment.

HRTEM image of CA-CDs showed CA-CDs are nearly spherical in shape (Fig. 1
(a)). The lattice spacing in Fig. 1 (b) is 0.199 nm, which is similar to the (102) crystal plane of the carbon structure (Shi, Su, Zhang, Huang, Liu, & Zhao, 2016).

The fluorescence of TA, the mixed solution of TA and CA-CDs, and the mixture of CA and CA-CDs is rather weak (Fig. 1 (d)). When AA was added to the mixed solution of TA and CA-CDs, the fluorescence of the system became strong at 430 nm, with excitation wavelength of 350 nm, which is similar to hydroxyterephthalate (TA-OH) (He, Lu, Gao, Song, Huang, Liu, & Liu, 2018; Wu, Gao, & Du, 2019). Hence, it is claimed the production of TA-OH in the TA-AA-CA-CDs system. It is speculated that CA-CDs have AAO mimetic activity and can catalyze the oxidation of AA with the generation of H2O2. The interaction of AA with H2O2 caused the production of hydroxyl radical (OH·), which changed TA to TA-OH with high fluorescence intensity.

AAO catalyzed the oxidation of AA to DHAA and accompanied by the production of H2O2 in the presence of O2. Hence, the role of oxygen, the catalytic oxidation product of AA (DHAA and H2O2) have been investigated to confirm the AAO-like activity of CA-CDs.

When the oxygen content in the TA-AA-CA-CDs system was reduced, the fluorescence intensity of the system decreased significantly (Fig. 2
(a)). The result reflected that dissolved oxygen is essential in the TA-AA-CA-CDs system.

OPDA reacted with DHAA to generate a product with strong fluorescent intensity. Hence, OPDA can be used as a fluorescent probe for DHAA. When AA coexisted with CA-CDs, the fluorescence of the system is greatly enhanced (Fig. 2 (b)). This indicated that CA-CDs owned AAO mimetic activity and can catalyze the oxidation of AA to form a large amount of DHAA. For comparison, there is only weak fluorescence from the AA-OPDA system. It is because that the self-oxidation of AA only produced a small amount of DHAA, which reacted with OPDA to produce weak fluorescence.

CAT could remove H2O2 by forming H2O and O2. When CAT is added to the CA-CDs-AA-TA system to confirm the production of H2O2 from the reaction between CA-CDs and AA. It is found that the fluorescent intensity of the solution is reduced with the concentration of the CAT (Fig. 2 (c)). This indicates that H2O2 is generated in the CA-CDs -AA-TA system·H2O2 plays an important role in the system. It has been reported that, in the presence of AA, the decomposition efficiency of H2O2 can be improved (Hou et al., 2018). We found the production of TA-OH in H2O2-AA-TA system (Fig. 2 (d)). The formation of OH• radicals in the CA-CDs-AA system was further verified by ESR ( Fig. S4 ). We rationalize that AA can be used as accelerator to accelerate the decomposition of H2O2 into OH• radicals.

The above results proved that CA-CDs own AAO mimetic activity. CA-CDs catalyzed the oxidation of AA to produce DHAA, accompanied by the generation of H2O2 in the presence of O2 (Scheme 1 ). Subsequently, AA accelerated the decomposition of H2O2 into a large number of OH• radicals. TA finally captured the OH• radicals to form TA-OH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The specificity of the method and actual sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Some possible interference substances, including lysine, proline, alanine, threonine, serine, cysteine, maltose, glutathione, glucose, NaCl, and KCl, were measured using this proposed method to verify the selectivity of this detection method for AA. The concentration of various amino acids and glutathione was 10 times higher than the concentration of AA, and the concentration of maltose, glucose, NaCl, and KCl was 100 times higher than the concentration of AA. The experimental results showed these interference substances showed negligible effects on the AA (1.5 mM) detection under the experimental conditions, which indicated that this method has an excellent specificity for the detection of AA (Fig. 3 (d)).

When the proposed method is applied to detect AA in juice and beverage samples, the detection results have no significant difference at the 95 % confidence level compared with the Chinese food safety standard method (Table 2 ), which proved that the proposed method is feasible for AA analysis in juice and beverage samples.

In summary, CA is used as the only raw material to synthesize CA-CDs by a one-step hydrothermal method. CA-CDs have excellent AAO-like activity and catalyze the oxidation of AA to produce DHAA and H2O2. AA can further accelerate the decomposition of H2O2 to produce OH•, and OH• can change TA to TA-OH with fluorescent emission. Based on this phenomenon, a turn-on mode detection sensor for AA has been developed and can be used for the detection of AA content in fruit juice and beverage samples. The natural enzyme-free method is simple to operate, and the one-step synthesized method for CA-CDs is facile and low-cost. The CA-CDs with excellent AAO mimetic activity and great robustness can be a new alternative to natural AAO. Furthermore, CA-CDs exhibited excellent biocompatibility and showed great potential applications in bioanalysis.

Zhen Lin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Qi Zeng: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Wensong Yao: Methodology, Data curation. Wei Chen: . Chuangui Cai: . Jialin Yang: Validation, Data curation. Xinhua Lin: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support from Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2020J01437, 2020J01629), Program for Young Top-notch Innovative Talents of Fujian Province of China (Fujian Commission Talent [2016] No. 11), and Talent Introduction Project Ningde Normal University (2020Y023).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.137928.
